name: Dagomba
description: The Dagomba are the ruling ethnic group of the Dagbon Kingdom — the most powerful traditional state in northern Ghana, founded by a Mamprussi prince in the 15th century. They are Muslim and their identity is shaped by the convergence of indigenous Gur traditions with Saharan Islamic court culture. The Dagbon Kingdom is still active today under the Ya-Na (king) in Yendi, and its culture — drumming, griots, and Islamic scholarship — is a bridge between forest Ghana and the Sahel.
cultural_highlights: Ya-Na (king) institution — the paramount chief of Dagbon whose elaborate enstoolment ceremony (Damba) is one of northern Ghana's most spectacular events
Damba festival — a celebration of the Prophet Muhammad's birth, featuring elaborate royal drumming and horsemanship
Dagbon court music (takai) — ensemble music combining talking drums, long trumpets, and royal praise poetry performed only by hereditary musicians
Griots (lunsi) — hereditary praise singers who maintain royal genealogies and historical epics spanning centuries

food_traditions.name: TZ (Tuo Zaafi)
food_traditions.description: Northern Ghana's ugali equivalent — fermented sorghum or millet dough eaten with ayoyo leaf soup; a daily staple

food_traditions.name: Peanut soup
food_traditions.description: Groundnut soup with dried fish and fermented locust beans (dawadawa) — a Sahel-influenced flavour profile common across northern Ghana

food_traditions.name: Shea butter cooking
food_traditions.description: Shea nut fat used in cooking replaces palm oil in many northern dishes; shea trees are sacred women's trees

music_traditions: Takai ensemble — a tightly choreographed dance with talking drums, flutes, and singing performed at royal courts
Talking drum (lunga) — a squeeze drum used by the lunsi griots to transmit royal messages and genealogies
Bamaaya — a harvest/water festival dance from the Tolon area, performed with calabash percussion
